content: A shaft carries a helical spur gear. Which one of the following bearings can NOT be used to support it?
explanation:
Option C – a straight (cylindrical) roller bearing.

• A helical gear imposes both a radial load and an axial thrust (due to the helix angle).

•

– Angular-contact bearings (A) are designed for combined radial and axial loads.

– Double-row ball bearings (B) can support moderate axial loads in both directions.

– Tapered roller bearings (D) carry heavy radial and axial loads.

•

Cylindrical (straight) roller bearings cannot absorb appreciable axial thrust, so they won’t support a helical gear’s axial load.

content: For a ball bearing, the fatigue life in millions of revolutions is given by $L = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^n$, where $P$ is the constant applied load and $C$ is the basic dynamic load rating. Which one of the following statements is TRUE?

options.content: $n = 3$, assuming that the inner racing is fixed and outer racing is revolving
$n = 1/3$, assuming that the inner racing is fixed and outer racing is revolving
$n = 3$, assuming that the outer racing is fixed and inner racing is revolving
$n = 1/3$, assuming that the outer racing is fixed and inner racing is revolving

explanation:
The fatigue life of a ball bearing, given by the formula $$L = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^n$$, depends on the nature of the contact and the relative motion between the races (inner and outer rings). The exponent $$n$$ is a crucial factor determined by empirical data and standard considerations in bearing design.

For ball bearings, the exponent $$n$$ is typically 3 when considering the conventional loading and rotation scenarios used in industry standards, as defined by organizations such as the American Bearing Manufacturers Association (ABMA) or the International Organization for Standardization (ISO). This value holds true for both cases where the inner race is fixed, and the outer race rotates, and vice versa.

Therefore, the correct statement regarding the exponent $$n$$ for ball bearings, assuming standardized loading and rotation conditions, would be:

Option A

$$n = 3$$, assuming that the inner racing is fixed and outer racing is revolving

And also:

Option C

$$n = 3$$, assuming that the outer racing is fixed and inner racing is revolving

Both these statements align with the industry-standard value of $$n = 3$$ for ball bearings under conventional conditions.

Hence, the true statement is:

Option C

$$n = 3$$, assuming that the outer racing is fixed and inner racing is revolving

content: A CNC worktable is driven in a linear direction by a lead screw connected directly to a stepper motor. The pitch of the lead screw is 5 mm. The stepper motor completes one full revolution upon receiving 600 pulses. If the worktable speed is 5 m/minute and there is no missed pulse, then the pulse rate being received by the stepper motor is
explanation:
To determine the pulse rate being received by the stepper motor, we first need to understand the relationship between the worktable speed, the lead screw pitch, and the stepper motor pulses.

Here is the given data:

  • Pitch of the lead screw: 5 mm
  • Stepper motor pulses per revolution: 600 pulses
  • Worktable speed: 5 m/minute

So, each revolution of the lead screw moves the worktable 5 mm.

Step 1: Convert worktable speed to the same unit of measurement

Worktable speed: 5 m/minute = 5000 mm/minute

Step 2: Calculate the number of lead screw revolutions per minute

Since each revolution moves the worktable 5 mm:

$$\text{Number of revolutions per minute} = \frac{\text{Worktable speed (mm/minute)}}{\text{Lead screw pitch (mm/revolution)}} = \frac{5000 \text{ mm/minute}}{5 \text{ mm/revolution}} = 1000 \text{ revolutions/minute}$$

Step 3: Calculate the total number of pulses per minute

The stepper motor receives 600 pulses per revolution:

$$\text{Pulses per minute} = \text{Number of revolutions per minute} \times \text{Pulses per revolution} = 1000 \text{ revolutions/minute} \times 600 \text{ pulses/revolution} = 600000 \text{ pulses/minute}$$

Step 4: Convert the pulse rate to pulses per second (Hz)

Since there are 60 seconds in a minute:

$$\text{Pulse rate} = \frac{600000 \text{ pulses/minute}}{60 \text{ seconds/minute}} = 10000 \text{ pulses/second} = 10 \text{ kHz}$$

So, the pulse rate being received by the stepper motor is 10 kHz.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option B: 10 kHz.

content: Which of the bearings given below SHOULD NOT be subjected to a thrust load?

Ball bearings are rated by a manufacturer for a life of $${10^6}$$  revolutions.  The catalogue rating of a particular bearing is $$16$$ $$kN.$$ If the design load is $$2$$ $$kN,$$ the life of the bearing will be $$P \times {10^6}$$ revolutions, where $$P$$ is equal to ___________.
Which one of the following is criterion in the design of hydrodynamic journal bearings?
The basic load rating of a ball bearing is

options.content: the maximum static radial load that can be applied without causing any plastic deformation of bearing components
the radial load at which $$90$$% of the group of apparently identical bearings run for one million revolutions before the first evidence of failure.
the maximum radial load that can be applied during operation without any plastic deformation of bearing components
a combination of radial and axial loads that can be applied without any plastic deformation

oil viscosity is to be increased
oil viscosity index is to be increased
oil viscosity index is to be decreased
